title: The Person and Work of Christ, Part 1
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 04-07-2024 - The Person and Work of Chist - Part 1 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Series Overview and the Westminster Larger Catechism
subpoints: Ten-week series on the Person and Work of Christ using Westminster Larger Catechism Questions 36-56
The Larger Catechism was written by the best group of English-language Bible scholars of its time (1647)
Topics planned: mediator of covenant of grace, two natures, offices of Christ, states of humiliation and exaltation, intercession, return
The Larger Catechism follows the structure of the Apostles' Creed and emphasizes the church community over individual use

outline.title: The Covenant of Works
subpoints: Question 20 of the Larger Catechism introduces the covenant of works
Life was promised to Adam and his posterity on condition of perfect and personal obedience
Adam was our federal/covenantal head — in his fall, all humanity fell
After the fall, Adam and humanity were no longer able to keep the covenant of works

outline.title: The Covenant of Grace
subpoints: Because man failed in the covenant of works, God establishes the covenant of grace through Christ
Christ becomes our representative in the covenant of grace, just as Adam was in the covenant of works
In the covenant of grace, we cannot fail — it is secured by Christ's perfect work, not our own
The covenant of grace is manifested by God freely providing a Mediator, requiring faith, and giving the Spirit
Even saving faith is a gift of God

outline.title: The Covenant of Redemption
subpoints: The covenant of works and the covenant of grace are both outworkings of an eternal covenant within the Trinity
In the covenant of redemption, Father, Son, and Spirit decreed that Jesus would be the Redeemer of the elect
The Son would obey the Father; the Spirit would apply the work to all the elect
Evidence: Ephesians 1:4 (chosen in Christ before the foundation of the world); 1 Peter 1:18-20 (foreordained before creation); John 10:29; Daniel 7:9-14

outline.title: Christ as Mediator of the Covenant of Grace
subpoints: Larger Catechism Q. 36: Christ is the only mediator of the covenant of grace
Christ brings God and man together through His person and work
The language of headship shows Christ as our representative before the Father
The language of substitution shows Christ standing in our place
The result: reconciliation, everlasting inheritance, the application of all salvation's benefits

summary: This is the first lesson in a planned ten-week Sunday school series on the Person and Work of Christ, guided by Westminster Larger Catechism Questions 36-56. Pastor Lyro begins by orienting the class to the Larger Catechism — its history, structure, and relationship to the Westminster Standards — before establishing the covenantal framework necessary to understand Christ as mediator. He introduces three covenants: the covenant of works, in which life was promised to Adam on condition of perfect obedience and which Adam, as federal/covenantal head of all humanity, broke; the covenant of grace, which God established after the fall, in which Christ becomes humanity's new representative and the covenant cannot fail because it is secured by Christ's perfect work rather than our own; and the covenant of redemption, the eternal intra-Trinitarian agreement in which the Father, Son, and Spirit planned the entire history of redemption before creation. Pastor Lyro draws on Ephesians 1, 1 Peter 1, John 10, and Daniel 7 to show that God's saving grace is directed to specific individuals chosen in Christ before the foundation of the world. The lesson concludes by framing Christ as the mediator of the covenant of grace — the One who brings God and man together through both His person and His work, standing in our place (substitution) and representing us as our Head (headship), so that all the blessings of the covenant are credited to the elect. The key conclusion: the heart of Scripture is that Christ died to save sinners — sinners who cannot save themselves.
transcript:
## Introduction: Series Overview and the Westminster Larger Catechism

The most important and satisfying subject for us to study, in the body, is the person and the work of Jesus Christ. The whole Bible points to Him. It's clear when you read Luke 24 — twice in that chapter, Jesus takes time to explain how all the different parts of the Bible pointed to Jesus: His work, who He is, and what He has done. And we're thankful that we have that in the Scriptures. And that's our plan to study for the next ten weeks, starting today — the person and the work of Jesus Christ.

We're going to use the Larger Catechism to guide us. This is not necessarily a series on the Larger Catechism; it's a series on the person and work of Christ. But the catechism is going to give us the scope and the sequence for our teaching. We're going to use the Larger Catechism, Questions 36 through 56, as a guide.

The Larger Catechism is one of three documents we hold as a church — alongside the Shorter Catechism and the Westminster Confession of Faith — that summarize what the Bible teaches. They are our subordinate standards of faith. The Bible is our primary and ultimate standard, and the catechisms and the confession are subordinate to it. It was written in 1647 alongside the Shorter Catechism, by the best group of Bible scholars in the English language available at the time — a gathering that I don't think would even happen today because of the ecumenicity of the council: Presbyterians, Episcopalians, Independents, Scottish commissioners. It was adopted by the Presbyterian Church of the United States of America in 1788 and by the Orthodox Presbyterian Church in 1938. Both ministers and ruling elders in the OPC take a vow that they believe in the system of doctrine contained in the catechisms.

As scholar Chad Van Dixhorn notes, both catechisms follow the traditional pattern of expanding on the Apostles' Creed. The Larger Catechism offers an ecclesial perspective — unlike the more individualist emphasis of the Shorter Catechism, the Larger Catechism accents the practical importance of church life for the Christian community.

Here is our plan for the ten weeks. Today we'll talk about Christ as mediator of the new covenant. Next week, why Christ needs to be both God and man. Then the names Jesus and Christ. Then the offices of Prophet, Priest, and King. Then Christ's states of humiliation and exaltation. Then His intercession. Then His return. The goal is to finish by June 9th.

## The Covenant of Works

We begin with Larger Catechism Question 36: "Who is the mediator of the covenant of grace?" The only mediator of the covenant of grace is the Lord Jesus Christ, who, being the eternal Son of God, of one substance and equal with the Father, in the fullness of time became man and so was and continues to be God and man in two entire distinct natures and one person forever.

But the catechism uses the term "covenant of grace" without defining it — because it was designed to be used alongside the confession, which does define it. So most of our time today will be understanding what the covenant of grace is so we can understand Christ's role in it.

To understand the covenant of grace, we also need to understand what is called the covenant of works. The Westminster Confession says, in Chapter 7, paragraph 2: "The first covenant made with men was a covenant of works, wherein life was promised to Adam and in him to his posterity, upon condition of perfect and personal obedience."

You may search for the word "covenant" in Genesis 1 and 2 and not find it. But Paul himself teaches that Adam represented all humanity before God — Romans 5:18-21: "Therefore, as through one man's offense, judgment came to all men, resulting in condemnation, even so through one man's righteous act, the free gift came to all men, resulting in justification of life. For as by one man's disobedience many were made sinners, so also by one man's obedience many will be made righteous."

And Hosea 6:7 makes a passing reference: "But like Adam they transgressed the covenant." So there was a covenant made with Adam.

God gave Adam a condition: don't eat of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. Since the condition was "if you do this, you die," we can assume the promise is "if you don't, you will live." Interesting that when Satan brings the temptation, he doesn't emphasize all the trees Adam could eat from — he emphasizes the one he cannot eat from. That's often how Satan operates: instead of letting us see all the blessings God provides, Satan focuses on that one little thing we want.

When Adam sinned, all humanity sinned with him because he represented us all — he was our federal head, our covenantal head. That's why humans are sinners before they sin. We don't become sinners when we sin — we sin because we are already sinners, because we fell all the way back with Adam. After the fall, Adam and humanity were no longer able to keep their part of the covenant of works. That ship had sailed.

## The Covenant of Grace

But God is gracious. And that's where the covenant of grace comes in. Larger Catechism Question 30: "Does God leave all mankind to perish in the state of sin and misery?" Answer: "God does not leave all men to perish in the state of sin and misery into which they fell by the breach of the first covenant, commonly called the covenant of works. But of His mere love and mercy, He elects some out of it and brings them into a state of salvation by the second covenant, commonly called the covenant of grace."

In the covenant of grace, Christ becomes our representative — the same way that in the covenant of works, Adam was our representative. Romans 5:18-21 again: "Through one man's offense, judgment came to all men, resulting in condemnation; even so through one man's righteous act, the free gift came to all men, resulting in justification of life."

The same way that Adam represented us in the covenant of works, Christ represents us in the covenant of grace. The same way that Adam was our federal head, Christ is our federal head. In the covenant of grace, we cannot fail — because it is not up to us; it's up to Christ. In the covenant of works, man could truly choose to sin or not to sin. In the covenant of grace, we enjoy God's blessings through Christ, and that cannot fail.

Larger Catechism Question 32: "How is the grace of God manifested in the second covenant?" Answer: "The grace of God is manifested in the second covenant, in that he freely provides and offers to sinners a mediator, and life and salvation by him; and requiring faith as the condition to interest them in him, promises and gives his Holy Spirit to all his elect, to work in them that faith, with all other saving graces; and to enable them unto all holy obedience, as the evidence of the truth of their faith and thankfulness to God."

Even saving faith is a gift of God. As Paul says in Ephesians 2:8-9: "For by grace you have been saved through faith, and that not of yourselves; it is the gift of God, not of works, lest anyone should boast." And verse 10: "For we are His workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand that we should walk in them." Even our sanctification, our good works, are prepared by God ahead of time and given to us. It's all grace.

## The Covenant of Redemption

Both the covenant of works and the covenant of grace are founded on the underlying covenant of redemption — a covenant made within the Trinity in eternity past. As historical theologian R. Scott Clark explains: "The covenant of grace is the historical working out of an eternal covenant of redemption — this so-called 'covenant before the covenant' — in which the members of the Holy Trinity decreed that Jesus would also be the Redeemer of those whom the Father had chosen in Him. In this covenant of redemption, the Holy Spirit will apply the work of Christ to all those whom the Father has chosen and for whom the Son will die."

You won't find the term "covenant of redemption" in the Bible — but the content is there. Ephesians 1:3-4: "Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ, just as He chose us in Him before the foundation of the world." God the Father chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world. 1 Peter 1:18-20: Christ "was foreordained before the foundation of the world but was manifest in these last times for you."

Jesus says in John 6:39: "This is the will of the Father who sent Me, that of all He has given Me I should lose nothing." And in John 10:28-29: "I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; neither shall anyone snatch them out of My hand. My Father, who has given them to Me, is greater than all; and no one is able to snatch them out of My Father's hand. I and My Father are one."

And in Daniel 7:9-14, Daniel has his heavenly vision of the Ancient of Days and the Son of Man — and most scholars believe that "Son of Man," Christ's favorite title for Himself in the New Testament, finds its origin here. The Son of Man comes before the Ancient of Days, and the Ancient of Days gives the kingdom to the Son of Man. And this is happening in eternity, before history flows from it.

So the covenant of works in history and the covenant of grace in all its administrations are God's way of working out in time what He planned in eternity past.

## Christ as Mediator of the Covenant of Grace

So Christ then is the mediator of the covenant of grace — that's where we started, that's where we were supposed to start the series. The Larger Catechism relies on the definition given in the Confession. And much of this series will be an explanation of how Christ exercises the office of mediator, because both His person and His work are involved.

The Confession says in Chapter 8, paragraph 5: "The Lord Jesus, by His perfect obedience and sacrifice of Himself, which He through the eternal Spirit once offered up unto God, hath fully satisfied the justice of His Father, and purchased not only reconciliation, but an everlasting inheritance in the kingdom of heaven, for all those whom the Father hath given unto Him."

He does that as a mediator — this is what He needs to do to bring us to the Father, to be the go-between. And in paragraph 8 the Confession continues: "To all those for whom Christ hath purchased redemption, He doth certainly and effectually apply and communicate the same; making intercession for them, and revealing unto them, in and by the word, the mysteries of salvation."

We see this in the language of headship — Ephesians 1:22-23: "He put all things under His feet and gave Him to be head over all things to the church, which is His body." And in the language of substitution — Ephesians 5:25: "Husbands, love your wives, just as Christ also loved the church and gave Himself for her." And 1 Peter 3:18: "For Christ also suffered once for sins, the just for the unjust, that He might bring us to God." And 2 Corinthians 5:21: "For He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in Him."

The covenant of grace is called a covenant of grace because it does not require our perfect obedience as the covenant of works did. It requires Christ's perfect obedience, which is then graciously counted by the Father as the obedience of the elect. In the covenant of works, Adam stood on his own merits. In the covenant of grace, the elect stand on the merits of Christ, counted to them through faith.

If you can get nothing else about the covenant of grace today, get this: the result of the covenant of grace is that Christ saves sinners. Sinners who cannot save themselves.

Father in heaven, thank you that we have a mediator before you — the only mediator between God and man, the man Jesus Christ, who stands before you and intercedes for us, who secured our salvation, who is our representative before you. We thank you for the grace that comes from that, and as we think about that, stir emotions and affections within us that drive us to worship you. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.
